behaviors.description: The cron parser validates syntax and rejects invalid expressions (invalid month, day, malformed syntax) with a descriptive error message.
behaviors.justification: Cron parsing is pure string validation logic with no I/O or external dependencies. Real failures—off-by-one bugs in range checks (month 13, day 32), malformed syntax detection—are purely computational and entirely testable in isolation with good test data. Mocking is unnecessary and not hiding any failures.
behaviors.test_description: Write unit tests that verify: (1) valid cron expressions parse successfully, (2) each invalid category is rejected (month >12, day >31, hour >23, malformed patterns), (3) error messages are descriptive and identify the invalid field. Cover boundary cases: month 13, day 32, day 30 in February, hour 25, etc. Use parametrized tests for comprehensive coverage.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: Unit level is sufficient because validation is deterministic and local; no mocking needed.

behaviors.description: The next-run-time calculator returns correct timestamps for standard cron expressions, including edge cases like 'last day of month' and 'nearest weekday.'
behaviors.justification: Time calculation is deterministic logic with no I/O or state. Real failures—leap year errors, off-by-one month boundaries, incorrect 'last day' or 'nearest weekday' logic—are computational bugs caught by Unit tests with varied cron patterns and date ranges. No external time source or mocking required.
behaviors.test_description: Create unit tests covering: (1) standard intervals (hourly, daily, monthly), (2) complex patterns (last day of month, nearest weekday, nth occurrence), (3) edge cases (Feb 29 in leap years, month transitions, century boundaries). Use parametrized tests with multiple date ranges. Test both single next-run and next-N-runs calculations.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: Unit level is sufficient because calculation is deterministic; no dependencies to hide bugs.

behaviors.description: The overlap detector identifies when two schedules would fire within the configured minimum gap (e.g., 5 minutes) and reports the conflicting time windows.
behaviors.justification: Overlap detection is pure interval comparison logic between pre-computed run-time sequences. Real failures—off-by-one errors in gap calculation, boundary condition bugs—are computational and testable in isolation. No I/O, no state mutations, no hidden dependencies.
behaviors.test_description: Write unit tests with schedule pairs covering: (1) no overlap (runs far apart), (2) overlap within gap (runs within min gap), (3) overlap at boundary (runs exactly at gap edge), (4) multiple overlaps. Verify reported conflict windows are correct. Test with various gap durations (1 min, 5 min, 1 hour). Use parametrized tests.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: Unit level is sufficient because comparison logic is deterministic; test data quality determines coverage.

behaviors.description: Timezone-aware scheduling produces correct run times across DST transitions. A job scheduled for 2:30 AM handles the spring-forward gap (2:30 AM doesn't exist) and fall-back ambiguity (2:30 AM occurs twice) correctly.
behaviors.justification: Real DST failures depend on the timezone library's actual DST rules, sourced from OS tzdata. A mocked timezone library would hide the real non-monotonic behavior during transitions—mocks return consistent results, masking bugs. Unit-level mock testing is insufficient. Integration with the real timezone library (reading actual tzdata) is required to catch real failures from incorrect DST handling.
behaviors.test_description: Create integration tests using real timezone library (e.g., pytz or zoneinfo) covering: (1) spring-forward transition (test jobs scheduled for non-existent 2:30 AM), (2) fall-back transition (test jobs scheduled for ambiguous 2:30 AM), (3) multiple timezones with different DST rules, (4) transitions in both hemispheres. Use actual transition dates from real tzdata. Verify correct handling of both the missing hour and the repeated hour.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: Mocking timezone behavior at Unit level would pass even with real bugs; Integration with real library is necessary.

behaviors.description: When a scheduled job's next run time has already passed (e.g., system downtime), the engine determines whether to skip or backfill based on the job's configured missed-run policy.
behaviors.justification: The behavior spans both decision (policy selection) and execution (applying policy to schedule state). Unit tests of policy decision logic alone would pass even if backfill generates missed times that conflict with other jobs, or if skip silently loses work. Real failures arise from the interaction between policy decision and schedule state. Integration testing with real local job/schedule state (in-memory DB or fixtures) is required to verify policy is correctly applied and produces consistent, non-conflicting schedule state.
behaviors.test_description: Create integration tests with real job/schedule fixtures covering: (1) skip policy—verify job's next_run advances without generating missed runs, (2) backfill policy—verify missed run times are computed correctly and added in order, (3) multiple jobs—verify backfilled runs do not conflict with other jobs' next runs, (4) edge cases—policy for job with no prior runs, policy change after missed run. Use in-memory DB or fixture state to test state consistency.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: Unit-level mocking of schedule state would hide ordering and conflict bugs; Integration with real state is necessary.
